The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>

The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

[[Category:Japanese archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Sleds]] [[Category:Transport equipment]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shura (sled)-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shura (sled)-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shura (sled)-qq]] [[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shura (sled)-qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shura (sled)-qq]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Shura (sled)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shura (sled)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Shura (sled)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Shura (sled)}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:修羅]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:qq]]

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}}

Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.

Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

revision.text:
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}}

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}}

The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.

The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.

<!--Shura (sled)-->[[Category:11]] [[Category:Shura (sled)]]<!--Shura (sled)--> [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Shura (sled)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:修羅]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shura (sled)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]]

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}}

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also! == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

revision.text:
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978年3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}}

Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year }}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.

== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|12=draft}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|12=draft}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}}
<!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--> <!--Shura (sled)-->
<!--Shura (sled)--> <!--Shura (sled)-->
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Sleds]] [[Category:Transport equipment]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shura (sled)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[en:Shura (sled)]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]] [[ja:修羅]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shura (sled)}}

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=draft}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|12=draft}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|12=draft}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|12=draft}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|12=draft}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世|12=draft}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=draft}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|12=draft}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=draft}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|12=draft}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|12=draft}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|12=draft}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|12=draft}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|12=draft}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|12=draft}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|12=draft}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|12=draft}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|12=draft}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|12=draft}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|12=draft}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|12=draft}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|12=draft}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|12=draft}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}}
<!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--> <!--Shura (sled)-->
<!--Shura (sled)--> <!--Shura (sled)-->
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Sleds]] [[Category:Transport equipment]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shura (sled)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[en:Shura (sled)]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]] [[ja:修羅]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shura (sled)}}
