56 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:修羅 (そり) up to rev 98073423
revision.text:
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|三ツ塚古墳の修羅の復元品（[[道明寺天満宮]]で保管されている）]] '''修羅'''（しゅら）は、重い[[石材]]などを運搬するために用いられた木製の大型[[そり|橇]]である。
==概要== 重機の存在しなかった時代に重いものを運ぶ重要な手段であった。[[コロ]]などの上に乗せることで、[[摩擦抵抗]]を減らすことができる。[[古墳時代]]には[[古墳]]の造営にも使用されていたと考えられているが、巨石運搬具を「修羅」と呼ぶようになったのは近世以降であり、古墳時代にこの名称は存在しない<ref>『大阪の史跡を訪ねて1 原始 古代篇』（ナンバー出版）</ref>。
VないしY字型の形状をしたものが発掘されている。二股に分かれている先端部を舟の舳先のように前方へ向け、これに切り出した石を乗せ、ロープで曳く。
[[近世]]の築城の様子を描いた「駿府城築城図屏風」などには、修羅で巨石を運搬する様子が描かれている。
==遺跡からの出土== [[ファイル:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|[[三ツ塚古墳]]出土 修羅（国の[[重要文化財]]）}}{{small|[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]展示。}}]] [[1978年]][[3月]]に[[大阪府]][[藤井寺市]]で[[三ツ塚古墳]]（道明寺六丁目）の周濠から、大小2つの修羅が出土。同年[[4月5日]]から[[4月6日|6日]]にかけてマスコミで大きく報じられ、[[4月15日]]の現地説明会には12,000人余りの人々が見学に訪れた<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html 長蛇の現地説明会(No.5)] - 藤井寺市ウェブサイト（修羅の話）</ref>。大きい修羅（全長8.8m、幅1.9m、重さ3.2トン）は[[アカガシ]]類の木、小さい修羅（全長2.8m、幅0.7m）は[[クヌギ]]類の木でできており、いずれも二股に分かれた一木であることがわかった。これらと同時に[[梃子|梃子棒]]（長さ6.2m、直径20cm）も発見された。出土場所などから、[[古市古墳群]]の最盛期だった[[5世紀]]ごろのものと推定されている。土中の[[地下水]]によりほぼ完全な形で保存されていたが、出土とともに急速な乾燥、劣化の恐れが心配された。そのため、同年5月に（財）[[元興寺文化財研究所]]（[[生駒市]]）へと運搬され、木材に含まれる水分の代わりに水溶性[[樹脂]]の[[ポリエチレングリコール]]を浸透させて固化する保存処理が行われることになった。同所には、長大な大修羅を収容できる設備はなく、この処理のために巨大な保存処理槽が新設された。
この発掘は大きな反響を呼び、同年[[9月3日]]には、[[朝日新聞社]]や[[考古学]]などの専門家によって、市内の[[大和川]]河川敷で、復元した修羅に巨石を乗せて牽引する実証実験が行われた。
大きい修羅よりも一足早く保存処理を終えた小さい修羅は[[1990年]]に「[[国際花と緑の博覧会]]」で展示された。保存処理が終わった修羅について、発掘した[[大阪府]]は、当時[[河南町]]に整備を進めていた風土記の丘に建設する博物館で展示する方針をとった。これに対して、出土地である藤井寺市と市民が反発。市長が藤井寺市に戻すよう府に要望したが認められなかった。現在、大きい修羅と梃子棒は[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]で展示され、府から貸与された小さい修羅が市立図書館で展示されている。
その他の出土例は次の通り。 * 石本遺跡（京都府福知山市） - 修羅形木製品（ミニチュア）1基。1984年（昭和59年）出土。 * [[鹿苑寺]]（京都府京都市） - 中世の修羅1基。1989年（平成元年）出土。 * 南古舘遺跡（福島県長沼町） - 中世の修羅1基。1988-1989年（昭和63-平成元年）出土。 * [[松面古墳]]（千葉県木更津市） - 古墳時代の修羅片1基。[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）出土<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "古墳時代のそり出土、石材を運搬か 木更津で国内２例目"]（朝日新聞、2019年5月4日記事）。</ref>。
==名前の由来== 大石をタイシャクと読み、それを[[帝釈天]]に引っ掛け、帝釈天を動かせるものは[[阿修羅]]すなわち修羅であると語呂合わせからきたものとされている<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html 修羅の由来(No.4)] - - 藤井寺市ウェブサイト（修羅の話）</ref>。つまり「修羅」の語は[[仏教]]が伝来し民衆に広く行き渡ったのちに生まれたものであり、古墳時代にこの器具がいかなる語で呼ばれていたかは定かではない。
==脚注== {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== *[[修羅 (林業)]]
== 外部リンク == *[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html 修羅の話] - 藤井寺市ウェブサイト
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[修羅 (そり)]] to [[Shura (sled)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|三ツ塚古墳の修羅の復元品（[[道明寺天満宮]]で保管されている）]] '''修羅'''（しゅら）は、重い[[石材]]などを運搬するために用いられた木製の大型[[そり|橇]]である。
==概要== 重機の存在しなかった時代に重いものを運ぶ重要な手段であった。[[コロ]]などの上に乗せることで、[[摩擦抵抗]]を減らすことができる。[[古墳時代]]には[[古墳]]の造営にも使用されていたと考えられているが、巨石運搬具を「修羅」と呼ぶようになったのは近世以降であり、古墳時代にこの名称は存在しない<ref>『大阪の史跡を訪ねて1 原始 古代篇』（ナンバー出版）</ref>。
VないしY字型の形状をしたものが発掘されている。二股に分かれている先端部を舟の舳先のように前方へ向け、これに切り出した石を乗せ、ロープで曳く。
[[近世]]の築城の様子を描いた「駿府城築城図屏風」などには、修羅で巨石を運搬する様子が描かれている。
==遺跡からの出土== [[ファイル:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|[[三ツ塚古墳]]出土 修羅（国の[[重要文化財]]）}}{{small|[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]展示。}}]] [[1978年]][[3月]]に[[大阪府]][[藤井寺市]]で[[三ツ塚古墳]]（道明寺六丁目）の周濠から、大小2つの修羅が出土。同年[[4月5日]]から[[4月6日|6日]]にかけてマスコミで大きく報じられ、[[4月15日]]の現地説明会には12,000人余りの人々が見学に訪れた<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html 長蛇の現地説明会(No.5)] - 藤井寺市ウェブサイト（修羅の話）</ref>。大きい修羅（全長8.8m、幅1.9m、重さ3.2トン）は[[アカガシ]]類の木、小さい修羅（全長2.8m、幅0.7m）は[[クヌギ]]類の木でできており、いずれも二股に分かれた一木であることがわかった。これらと同時に[[梃子|梃子棒]]（長さ6.2m、直径20cm）も発見された。出土場所などから、[[古市古墳群]]の最盛期だった[[5世紀]]ごろのものと推定されている。土中の[[地下水]]によりほぼ完全な形で保存されていたが、出土とともに急速な乾燥、劣化の恐れが心配された。そのため、同年5月に（財）[[元興寺文化財研究所]]（[[生駒市]]）へと運搬され、木材に含まれる水分の代わりに水溶性[[樹脂]]の[[ポリエチレングリコール]]を浸透させて固化する保存処理が行われることになった。同所には、長大な大修羅を収容できる設備はなく、この処理のために巨大な保存処理槽が新設された。
この発掘は大きな反響を呼び、同年[[9月3日]]には、[[朝日新聞社]]や[[考古学]]などの専門家によって、市内の[[大和川]]河川敷で、復元した修羅に巨石を乗せて牽引する実証実験が行われた。
大きい修羅よりも一足早く保存処理を終えた小さい修羅は[[1990年]]に「[[国際花と緑の博覧会]]」で展示された。保存処理が終わった修羅について、発掘した[[大阪府]]は、当時[[河南町]]に整備を進めていた風土記の丘に建設する博物館で展示する方針をとった。これに対して、出土地である藤井寺市と市民が反発。市長が藤井寺市に戻すよう府に要望したが認められなかった。現在、大きい修羅と梃子棒は[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]で展示され、府から貸与された小さい修羅が市立図書館で展示されている。
その他の出土例は次の通り。 * 石本遺跡（京都府福知山市） - 修羅形木製品（ミニチュア）1基。1984年（昭和59年）出土。 * [[鹿苑寺]]（京都府京都市） - 中世の修羅1基。1989年（平成元年）出土。 * 南古舘遺跡（福島県長沼町） - 中世の修羅1基。1988-1989年（昭和63-平成元年）出土。 * [[松面古墳]]（千葉県木更津市） - 古墳時代の修羅片1基。[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）出土<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "古墳時代のそり出土、石材を運搬か 木更津で国内２例目"]（朝日新聞、2019年5月4日記事）。</ref>。
==名前の由来== 大石をタイシャクと読み、それを[[帝釈天]]に引っ掛け、帝釈天を動かせるものは[[阿修羅]]すなわち修羅であると語呂合わせからきたものとされている<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html 修羅の由来(No.4)] - - 藤井寺市ウェブサイト（修羅の話）</ref>。つまり「修羅」の語は[[仏教]]が伝来し民衆に広く行き渡ったのちに生まれたものであり、古墳時代にこの器具がいかなる語で呼ばれていたかは定かではない。
==脚注== {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== *[[修羅 (林業)]]
== 外部リンク == *[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html 修羅の話] - 藤井寺市ウェブサイト

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
{{Japanese-history-stub|しゆら}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しゆら}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shura (sled)}}
{{translated page|ja|修羅 (そり)|version=98073423|comment=Imported full ja history}}

By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>

The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

[[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[en:Shura (sled)]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]] [[ja:修羅]]
<!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)-->

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

[[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[en:Shura (sled)]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]] [[ja:修羅]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|qq=}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|qq=}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|qq=}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koro|qq=}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|qq=}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiidera|qq=}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefecture|qq=}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 5|qq=}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 15|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/5th century|qq=}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikoma|qq=}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 3|qq=}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato River|qq=}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|qq=}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinkaku-ji|qq=}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishakuten|qq=}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashura|qq=}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism|qq=}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|qq=}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|qq=}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|qq=}}.

It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|qq=}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiidera|qq=}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefecture|qq=}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 5|qq=}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 15|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/5th century|qq=}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikoma|qq=}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 3|qq=}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato River|qq=}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|qq=}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinkaku-ji|qq=}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishakuten|qq=}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashura|qq=}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism|qq=}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|qq=}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|qq=}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|qq=}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koro|qq=}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|近世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|qq=}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|ja|藤井寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiidera|qq=}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefecture|qq=}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 5|qq=}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|ja|4月15日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 15|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/5th century|qq=}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} in {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikoma|qq=}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 3|qq=}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato River|qq=}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|qq=}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinkaku-ji|qq=}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishakuten|qq=}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashura|qq=}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism|qq=}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|ja|9月3日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 3|qq=}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato River|qq=}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|ja|国際花と緑の博覧会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|qq=}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|qq=}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|ja|鹿苑寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinkaku-ji|qq=}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Taishakuten|ja|帝釈天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishakuten|qq=}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Ashura|ja|阿修羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashura|qq=}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism|qq=}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|qq=}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|qq=}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|qq=}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koro|qq=}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the [[:en:Early modern period|Edo period]] depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}}{{small|On display at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]].}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|qq=}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The discovery received extensive media coverage on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on [[:en:April 15|April 15]].<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the [[:en:5th century|5th century]], the peak of the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} in [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on [[:en:September 3|September 3]], where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the [[:en:Expo '90|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition]]. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]]. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * [[:en:Kinkaku-ji|Kinkaku-ji]] (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in [[:en:2014|2014]].<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with [[:en:Śakra (Buddhism)|Taishakuten]] (Śakra), who is opposed by [[:en:Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]]. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * [[:en:Kinkaku-ji|Kinkaku-ji]] (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in [[:en:2014|2014]].<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with [[:en:Śakra (Buddhism)|Taishakuten]] (Śakra), who is opposed by [[:en:Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

[[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[en:Shura (sled)]] [[sn:Shura (sled)]] [[ja:修羅]]
<!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--> [[Category:そり]] [[Category:日本の考古資料]]

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|qq=}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|qq=}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|qq=}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koro|qq=}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the [[:en:Early modern period|Edo period]] depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}}{{small|On display at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]].}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|qq=}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The discovery received extensive media coverage on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on [[:en:April 15|April 15]].<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the [[:en:5th century|5th century]], the peak of the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} in [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on [[:en:September 3|September 3]], where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the [[:en:Expo '90|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition]]. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]]. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * [[:en:Kinkaku-ji|Kinkaku-ji]] (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in [[:en:2014|2014]].<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with [[:en:Śakra (Buddhism)|Taishakuten]] (Śakra), who is opposed by [[:en:Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website

What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

[[ja:%E4%BF%AE%E7%BE%85 (%E3%81%9D%E3%82%8A)]] [[ja:&action=edit]]
[[sn:Shura (sled)]] [[ja:修羅]]
<!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--><!--Shura (sled)--> [[Category:そり]] [[Category:日本の考古資料]] [[Category:Vehicles]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:修羅 (そり)]]

[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|qq=}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to そり}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=Building Stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|qq=}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koro|qq=}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the [[:en:Early modern period|Edo period]] depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}}{{small|On display at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]].}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujiidera]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The discovery received extensive media coverage on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on [[:en:April 15|April 15]].<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the [[:en:5th century|5th century]], the peak of the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]]. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} in [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on [[:en:September 3|September 3]], where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|qq=}} organized an experiment on the banks of the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the [[:en:Expo '90|1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition]]. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum]]. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * [[:en:Kinkaku-ji|Kinkaku-ji]] (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in [[:en:2014|2014]].<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with [[:en:Śakra (Buddhism)|Taishakuten]] (Śakra), who is opposed by [[:en:Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shura (Forestry)|ja|修羅 (林業)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shura (Forestry)|qq=}} * {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/index.html "The Story of Shura"] - Fujiidera City Website
