Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Gangoji Institute for Research of Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11388045}} in {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma|1=Ikoma, Nara}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|en|September 3|WD=Q2860|lt=September 3|1=September 3}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|1=Asahi Shimbun Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q444649}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|en|Yamato River|WD=Q9001527|lt=Yamato River|1=Yamato River}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|Expo '90|en|Expo '90|WD=Q903645|lt=1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|1=Expo '90}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|en|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|WD=Q4557237|lt=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|1=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|en|Kinkaku-ji|WD=Q270983|lt=Kinkaku-ji|1=Kinkaku-ji}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014|1=2014}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Śakra (Buddhism)|en|Śakra (Buddhism)|WD=Q1444745|lt=Taishakuten|1=Śakra (Buddhism)}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Asura (Buddhism)|en|Asura (Buddhism)|WD=Q1637262|lt=Ashura|1=Asura (Buddhism)}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:修羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:修羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:修羅]]
[[Image:Domyojitenmangu syura(fukugen).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Reconstructed ''shura'' from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (housed at {{ill|Dōmyōji Tenmangū|ja|道明寺天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyōji Tenmangū|1=Dōmyōji Tenmangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641744}})]] '''Shura''' (修羅) is a large wooden {{ill|sled|ja|橇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sled|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to そり|1=Sled|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q181388}} used for transporting heavy {{ill|stone materials|ja|石材|1=building stone|2=ja|3=石材|4=ko|5=석재|6=fr|7=Pierre à bâtir|8=nl|9=Bouwsteen|10=it|11=Pietra (classificazione commerciale)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone materials|13=User:Immanuelle/Building Stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2314751}}.
== Overview == Shura served as a crucial means of transporting heavy objects in times when heavy machinery did not exist. By placing it on rollers ({{ill|koro|ja|コロ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koro|1=Q11302857|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11302857}}), friction resistance could be reduced. It is believed that shura was used for the construction of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, though the term "shura" for these stone transport tools only appeared in the early modern period and was not used during the Kofun period itself.<ref>''Visiting Osaka’s Historical Sites Vol. 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Periods'' (Number Publishing).</ref>
Excavated shura exhibit V- or Y-shaped designs. Stones were placed on the split front ends resembling the prow of a boat, which were then dragged using ropes.
The "Sunpu Castle Construction Folding Screen" from the {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|WD=Q5308718|lt=Edo period|1=Early modern period}} depicts the use of shura for transporting large stones.
== Excavations == [[File:三ツ塚古墳出土 修羅.JPG|thumb|280px|right|{{center|Shura excavated from {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}})}}{{small|On display at {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|en|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|WD=Q4557237|lt=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|1=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum}}.}}]] In {{ill|March 1978|ja|1978th year 3月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 1978|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, two shura, one large and one small, were excavated from the moat surrounding {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} (Dōmyōji Rokuchōme) in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujiidera|1=Fujiidera}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The discovery received extensive media coverage on {{ill|April 5|en|April 5|WD=Q2507|lt=April 5|1=April 5}}, and over 12,000 people attended the on-site explanation on {{ill|April 15|en|April 15|WD=Q2497|lt=April 15|1=April 15}}.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354480374.html "A Long Line at the On-site Explanation (No. 5)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
The large shura (8.8 meters long, 1.9 meters wide, and weighing 3.2 tons) was made from a species of oak (''akagashi''), while the smaller shura (2.8 meters long, 0.7 meters wide) was made from ''kunugi'' oak. Both were carved from single logs with bifurcated ends. Alongside these, a lever (6.2 meters long, 20 cm in diameter) was also discovered. Based on their excavation site and other evidence, these artifacts are estimated to date back to the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}}, the peak of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Cluster|1=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}}. The shura were well-preserved due to the groundwater in the soil but were at risk of rapid drying and deterioration after excavation. Consequently, in May of the same year, they were transferred to the {{ill|Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|元興寺文化財研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gangoji Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Gangoji Institute for Research of Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11388045}} in {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma|1=Ikoma, Nara}} for preservation treatment using polyethylene glycol to solidify the water-soluble resin within the wood. The institute constructed a massive preservation tank specifically to accommodate the large shura.
The excavation garnered significant public interest, culminating in a large-scale demonstration on {{ill|September 3|en|September 3|WD=Q2860|lt=September 3|1=September 3}}, where experts and the {{ill|Asahi Shimbun|ja|朝日新聞社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shimbun|1=Asahi Shimbun Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q444649}} organized an experiment on the banks of the {{ill|Yamato River|en|Yamato River|WD=Q9001527|lt=Yamato River|1=Yamato River}} to haul a large stone using a reconstructed shura.
The smaller shura, which underwent preservation treatment earlier, was displayed at the {{ill|Expo '90|en|Expo '90|WD=Q903645|lt=1990 International Garden and Greenery Exposition|1=Expo '90}}. Despite local opposition from Fujiidera City, where the artifacts were unearthed, the larger shura and lever are now displayed at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|en|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|WD=Q4557237|lt=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu-Asuka Museum|1=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum}}. The smaller shura is loaned to the Fujiidera City Library for exhibition.
Other notable discoveries include: * {{ill|Ishimoto Site|ja|石本遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishimoto Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture): One miniature shura-shaped wooden artifact, excavated in 1984. * {{ill|Kinkaku-ji|en|Kinkaku-ji|WD=Q270983|lt=Kinkaku-ji|1=Kinkaku-ji}} (Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated in 1989. * {{ill|Minamikotachi Site|ja|南古舘遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikotachi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Nagahama Town, Fukushima Prefecture): One medieval shura, excavated from 1988 to 1989. * {{ill|Matsumen Kofun|ja|松面古墳|1=Matsumen Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumen Kofun|qq=}} (Kisarazu City, Chiba Prefecture): One fragment of a Kofun-period shura, excavated in {{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014|1=2014}}.<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM526THKM52UCLV007.html "Sled from the Kofun Period Discovered: Likely Used for Stone Transport, Second Example in Japan," Asahi Shimbun, May 4, 2019.]</ref>
== Etymology == The name "shura" is thought to derive from a wordplay: the Japanese reading of "great stone" as ''taishaku'' is associated with {{ill|Śakra (Buddhism)|en|Śakra (Buddhism)|WD=Q1444745|lt=Taishakuten|1=Śakra (Buddhism)}} (Śakra), who is opposed by {{ill|Asura (Buddhism)|en|Asura (Buddhism)|WD=Q1637262|lt=Ashura|1=Asura (Buddhism)}} (Asura). The term "shura" thus emerged as a later association, after the introduction of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} into Japan. What this tool was called during the Kofun period remains unknown.<ref>[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/rekishikanko/kodaikaranomesseji/shuranohanashi/1387354060782.html "Origins of Shura (No. 4)" - Fujiidera City Website (Shura Story)]</ref>
